mnemonics.title: Frequency Analysis Priority
mnemonics.content: Etaoin Shrdlu - Remember the 12 most common letters in English order: E-T-A-O-I-N-S-H-R-D-L-U. This phrase helps recall the frequency order for cryptanalysis.

mnemonics.title: Key Properties
mnemonics.content: MONO: M-One-N-O - One mapping for all occurrences. Each Plaintext letter has One corresponding Ciphertext letter throughout the entire message.

mnemonics.title: Permutation Count
mnemonics.content: 26 Factorial = 4×10²⁶ keys. That's 40 billion billion billion - way more than grains of sand on Earth, yet still broken by counting!

acronyms.expansion: Symmetric Group of degree 26 - The mathematical group containing all 26! permutations of the alphabet
acronyms.usage: Used to formally define monoalphabetic cipher as σ ∈ S₂₆ where σ is a permutation from the set {0,1,...,25} to itself

acronyms.expansion: Chosen Plaintext Attack - An attack model where attacker can obtain ciphertexts for chosen plaintexts
acronyms.usage: Monoalphabetic cipher is completely vulnerable to CPA; just 26 plaintext-ciphertext pairs reveal the entire key

acronyms.expansion: Known Plaintext Attack - An attack where attacker has some plaintext-ciphertext pairs
acronyms.usage: With just a few known pairs, the key can be partially or fully recovered for monoalphabetic ciphers

visualTips.visualization: Imagine 26 boxes labeled A-Z with balls. A key is one specific way to shuffle balls into boxes. Each ball's final box is its ciphertext letter. The inverse key shows which ball was in each box.

Two bar charts side-by-side: one showing standard English frequencies (tall bars at E,T,A), one showing ciphertext frequencies. Match the patterns - tallest to tallest, second to second - to deduce mappings.
Overlay frequency grids showing common letter pairs. In ciphertext, 'XY' appearing often likely corresponds to common pairs like 'TH', 'HE', 'IN'. Use these to refine mappings iteratively.

analogies.analogy: Like a code book where each word has ONE fixed code number. If you know 'apple' = 42, it always means 'apple'. With enough coded messages, patterns emerge and the code book can be cracked.

Like recognizing a song from just hearing a few distinctive notes. The distinctive frequency of certain letters (like E being very common) acts as your 'musical notes' to identify the 'song' (plaintext).
A bank vault with 10⁰²⁶ possible combinations seems unbreakable, but if there's a window (frequency analysis) that lets you see inside, the lock complexity becomes irrelevant.
